blurb: Tight green rosettes laced tip-to-tip with fine white webbing, like a dusting of cobweb spun across the top. Properly cold-hardy — this is one that could actually live outdoors through a real winter.
size: Rosettes ~1–2 in, offsetting into a mat

care.light: Full sun to very bright light keeps the rosettes tight and the webbing dense; in shade they open up and the cobweb thins.
care.water: Soak-and-dry in growth; it's a mountain plant used to sharp drainage and dry spells. Keep water out of the woolly crown.
care.temperature: Genuinely hardy — shrugs off frost and can overwinter outdoors in cold zones; its enemy is heat plus wet, not cold.
care.dormancy: Grows spring through summer and rests over winter; it just needs to stay dry when cold.
care.feeding: Little to none — lean conditions suit it.
care.potting: Shallow pots are fine; it spreads into a mat of offsets you can divide any time.
care.propagation: Pull the 'chick' offsets, which are usually already rooting, and pot them up.
care.bloom: The mother rosette is monocarpic — it sends up a starry pink flower spike, then dies, replaced by its ring of chicks.
care.troubles: Rot from heat-and-wet or water sitting in the crown; otherwise very tough.
care.toxicity: Not considered toxic to cats or dogs — a genuinely pet-safe houseleek. The only note of caution is that the leaf sap can mildly irritate skin in some people, but it's no danger to pets.

sources.title: Sempervivum (Hens and Chicks / Houseleek) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
